supporting_evidence: Most common cause of fever with rash in preschool-age children (accounts for ~70% of cases)
Acute onset of rash with concurrent fever is classic presentation
Age 4 is peak incidence for many viral exanthems (roseola, enteroviral, adenoviral)
Lethargy and anorexia are common with viral febrile illness in young children
Sandpaper-like rash with fever in preschool-age child is classic presentation
Peak incidence age 4-8 years
High fever (39.8°C) is consistent
Requires rapid strep test and antibiotic treatment to prevent rheumatic fever
Fever with rash and lethargy raises concern for invasive meningococcal infection
Rapid-onset rash is characteristic — petechial/purpuric rash can evolve within hours
Tachycardia (HR 185) and borderline BP (85/50) may indicate early sepsis
Age under 5 is a high-risk group for meningococcal disease

candidates.diagnosis: Urinary tract infection with viral rash
supporting_evidence: UTI is a common occult source of fever in young children
Rash may be coincidental viral exanthem with concurrent UTI
Urinalysis should be performed in all febrile children under 5 without clear source
Anorexia and lethargy can be presenting signs of UTI in preschool children
Fever with rash in child aged 6 months to 5 years is within classic age range
Requires ≥5 days of fever plus additional criteria (conjunctivitis, oral changes, extremity changes, lymphadenopathy)
Incomplete Kawasaki should be considered if fever persists beyond 5 days
Coronary artery aneurysm risk if untreated — early IVIG is protective

reasoning: 4-year-old female with acute fever and rash. The differential is dominated by viral exanthem as the statistically most likely cause. Scarlet fever is second given the age group and presentation. Meningococcal disease must be actively excluded given the combination of fever, rash, lethargy, and borderline vital signs — this is a don't-miss diagnosis where early recognition is life-saving. UTI should be screened for as an occult fever source. Kawasaki disease is lower probability given the short duration of symptoms but should be reconsidered if fever persists.
